coredata.dc:title: Outstanding lubrication properties of carbon dot-based ionic liquids
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haocheng
Li, Yi
Zhang, Songwei
Che, Qinglun
Hu, Litian
Zhang, Jianjun
coredata.dc:description: Although carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention due to their excellent lubricity and high load-carrying capacity as lubricant additives, the deficiency of easy agglomeration and the feature of solid state still restrict the application of CDs in the field of lubrication engineering. In order to break through the above limitations, novel liquid CD materials, carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs), were synthesized via ionic modification of CDs with organic ammonium cations endowing new functions as novel liquid lubricants, which demonstrates that CDILs provide better lubrication performance than classical ILs ([BMIM][BF4]) and at room and high temperature (30 °C and 100 °C). More importantly, the newly synthesized [N888H][CDs] containing tertiary ammonium cations with long alkyl chains exhibits the best lubrication performance. The resultant analyses give direct evidence that CD anions play a surprising role in improving the lubricity and protecting the steel/steel friction pairs, growing robust tribofilm with abundant CDs on the worn surfaces. Hence, it can not only offer fundamental insights into the formation of the tribofilm, but also provide a method for modifying CDs to promote their lubricity as liquid lubricants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ionic modification
Ionic liquids
Tribological behavior
Tribofilm

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of CDs and CDILs

Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51975559), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2020ME135)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis procedures and the molecular structures of CDILs.
(a) TEM image (inset: the size distribution of CDs), (b) HR-TEM image.
The FT-IR spectra of CA, CDs and CDILs.
(a) UV–vis spectra of CA, CDs and CDILs, the insert image is CDs under sunlight (left) and hand-held UV lamp of 365 nm light (right), (b) TGA curves of CDs, [BMIM][BF4] and CDILs.
(a) Friction coefficient curves, (b) Wear volumes and average friction coefficient (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 30 °C, duration of 30 min).

SEM images of wear scars of steel disk lubricated by [BMIM][BF4] and CDILs corresponding to (a-b) [BMIM][BF4], (c-d) [N444H][CDs], (e-f) [N4444][CDs], (g-h) [N888H][CDs] (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 30 °C, duration of 30 min).
(a) Friction coefficient curves, (b) Wear volumes and average friction coefficient (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 100 °C, duration of 30 min).

SEM images of wear scars of disk lubricated by [BMIM][BF4] and CDILs corresponding to (a-b) [BMIM][BF4], (c-d) [N444H][CDs], (e-f) [N4444][CDs], (g-h) [N888H][CDs] (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 100 °C, duration of 30 min).
Friction coefficient curves lubricated by [N888H][CDs] (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 100 °C, duration of 120 min).
The XPS spectra of worn surface lubricated by [N888H][CDs] (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 100 °C, duration of 120 min).

(a-b, e-f) cross-section TEM images, (c) EDS elemental maps of wear scar of disk lubricated by [N888H][CDs], (d) EDS line-scan analyses along the line given in (c) (Test condition: load of 200 N, temperature of 100 °C, duration of 120 min).
Schematic illustration of lubrication mechanism of CDILs.
The kinematic viscosity of CDILs and [BMIM][BF4].
The EDS analysis of typical element content (%) on unworn surface and worn surface lubricated by [N888H][CDs] with different test durations.

head.ce:title: Outstanding lubrication properties of carbon dot-based ionic liquids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical & Automotive Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao 266033, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical & Automotive Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao 266033, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Resource Chemistry & New Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qingdao Center of Resource Chemistry & New Materials, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China (S. Zhang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention due to their excellent lubricity and high load-carrying capacity as lubricant additives, the deficiency of easy agglomeration and the feature of solid state still restrict the application of CDs in the field of lubrication engineering. In order to break through the above limitations, novel liquid CD materials, carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs), were synthesized via ionic modification of CDs with organic ammonium cations endowing new functions as novel liquid lubricants, which demonstrates that CDILs provide better lubrication performance than classical ILs ([BMIM][BF4]) and at room and high temperature (30 °C and 100 °C). More importantly, the newly synthesized [N888H][CDs] containing tertiary ammonium cations with long alkyl chains exhibits the best lubrication performance. The resultant analyses give direct evidence that CD anions play a surprising role in improving the lubricity and protecting the steel/steel friction pairs, growing robust tribofilm with abundant CDs on the worn surfaces. Hence, it can not only offer fundamental insights into the formation of the tribofilm, but also provide a method for modifying CDs to promote their lubricity as liquid lubricants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon dots (CDs) are nano-particles with a size below 10 nm mainly consisting of carbon [1]. As a result of the unique structures, special physicochemical properties and great biocompatibility, CDs have gained more attentions and applications in various fields since being discovered in 2004 [2–4], such as bioimaging [5], chemical additive [6–8], catalyzing [9], lubrication [10–13] and so on. Over the past decade, many methods and precursors had been used to synthesize the CDs [10,14–19]. For lubrication field, small size CDs surrounded with the functional group of amidogen, carboxyl and hydroxyl can easily adsorb on the friction surface to form protective film [20]. However, CDs tend to aggregate, limiting their widespread application in lubrication engineering. To improve the lubricating properties and applications, CDs were further functionalized with organics, inorganics or polymeric materials through surface groups [11,21–27]. For example, the oleylamine was used to modify the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), decreasing the polarity of CQDs, which could increase their solubility in poly alpha olefin (PAO) oil. Furthermore, the carbon dots deposited on the surface to form a protective film, enhancing the lubricating property and loading capacity of PAO [11]. The tertiary amine-terminated carbon dots with reversible CO2 switchable amphiphilicity were used as versatile lubricant additives, broadening the lubricating application range of CDs [22]. The CDs-PEI0.6-10
k was synthesized by citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) using hydrothermal treatments, which exhibited the excellent friction-reducing and anti-wear performance as additives in polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG200) [26]. Although the organics and polymeric materials as modified materials could enhance the lubricating performance of CDs, the more excellent materials and methods need to be explored.

Recently, the studies on modified materials are more focused on the ionic liquids (ILs). ILs are considered as excellent lubricants or lubricating additives for their outstanding properties, such as great thermal stability, nonflammability, low vapor pressure, structural designability and high ionic conductivity [28–31]. For example, the 1-methyl-3-hexylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([BMIM][BF4]) was first used as lubricants in lubrication field in 2001 and exhibited superior tribological behaviors as compared with phosphazene (X-1P) and perfluoropolyether (PFPE) lubricants [32]. The phosphonium-based ionic liquids were used as lubricants and lubricant additives of multialkylated cyclopentanes (MACs) in vacuum environment, performing great lubricating properties [29]. The 4, 6-dimethyl-2-mercaptopyrimidine (DMMP) based ionic liquids showed excellent lubrication performance as lubricants in comparison with [BMIM][BF4] [33]. To our best knowledge, the electrons will escape the steel surface during the friction process, and thus anions first adsorb on the surface [34–36]. On account of the unique ionic structure for ILs, they exhibit stronger adsorption capacity on the surface. As a result, ILs were often chosen as good modifiers of CDs to increase their adsorption capacity on the friction surfaces or to improve their solubility in base oil. Recently, the combinations of ILs/CDs were focused on the covalent modifying approach. For instance, Zhao et al. [37] synthesized the carbon dots-ionic liquid (CDs-IL) hybrid nanoparticles by in-situ carbonization, which could play the synergistic lubrication effect. Therefore, the tribological performance of PEG was improved significantly. Wang et al. [12] used a one-pot pyrolysis way to synthesize the 1-aminopropyl-3-methyl-imidazolium bromide capped carbon dots (CDs-Br), and afterwards the CDs-NTf2 were obtained by the anion exchange reaction between Br− and NTf2 −. The friction coefficient and wear scar diameter reduced by 70 % and 33 %, respectively, when CDs-NTf2 were introduced into the PEG base oil as additives. What’s more, Mou et al. [38] prepared the poly(ionic liquid)s brush-grafted carbon dots (CDs-PILs) for the first time by polymerization reaction, which were expected to be used as a high-performance lubricant additive of PEG200.

From researches above, the covalent modification was considered as a good way to reduce the aggregation and improve the adsorption capacity of CDs. However, the covalent modification method was complex and energy-intensive. Moreover, the covalent modified CDs in previous researches were solid state at room temperature, which obviously limited the widespread application of CDs as lubricants or lubricating additives. Based on these, we propose the ionic modification approach to use CDs as anionic moieties of ILs directly.

In this work, three novel carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs) were obtained via ionic modification, using CDs as unique anions. The lubrication performance of CDILs as lubricants was investigated under a load of 200 N at different temperatures and compared with the classical ILs ([BMIM][BF4]). Finally, the lubrication mechanism of CDILs were discussed and revealed through a series of surface analyses.

Citric acid anhydrous (CA) was purchased from Tianjin Aopusheng Chemical ltd. Trioctylamine (TOA, 95 %) and tetrabutylammonium hydroxide (H2O content of 25 wt%) were supplied by the Aladdin Reagent Co., ltd (China). Tributylamine (TBA, 99 %) was employed by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([BMIM][BF4]) was obtained from the Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. The deionized water prepared with an ultrapure water purifier in our lab. All the chemicals were afforded as received without further purification.

The CDs were prepared by the direct pyrolyzation of CA. Briefly, 10 g CA was introduced into a 50 mL beaker which was immersed in an oil bath and then heated up to 210 °C. During the heating process, CA changed slowly from yellow liquid to orange. When the products’ color became total orange (about 30 min), stopped heating and dissolved the products in deionized water with the help of ultrasonic dispersion. After that, the ethyl acetate was used to extract CDs from the orange dispersion which could remove the unreacted CA, and then the deionized water was poured into the ethyl acetate solution. At last, the CDs aqueous solution was obtained by removing the ethyl acetate through rotatory evaporation (yield: about 1.5 g CDs per 10 g CA).

In this work, three CDILs, [N444H][CDs], [N4444][CDs] and [N888H][CDs], were prepared. Fig. 1 shows the synthesis process and the molecular structures of CDILs. For the [N444H][CDs] and [N888H][CDs], the aqueous solution of CDs was placed into a round-bottom flask. Excess amount of tributylamine or trioctylamine was added into the flask under continuous stirring for 24 h at the room temperature to ensure CDs were completely consumed. Subsequently, the crude products were washed with petroleum ether and then the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation. Finally, the products were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

For the [N4444][CDs], the aqueous solution of CDs was placed into a round-bottom flask. Then tetrabutyl ammonium hydroxide was added dropwise in the flask until the pH value reached 7 and the solution was stirred continuously for 6 h at room temperature. Finally, the water was removed by rotary evaporation and the products were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The kinematic viscosity of CDILs and [BMIM][BF4] is displayed in Table 1 . The viscosity of CDILs is significantly higher than [BMIM][BF4] at 40 °C. The structures of CDILs were characterized by HRMS ( Figs. S1
to S3). The m/z results are 186.2220 ([N444H]+), 242.2845 ([N4444]+) and 354.4101 ([N888H]+), respectively.

The microstructure images of CDs were obtained by the JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR Bruker TENSOR + 27) was used to characterize the chemical structures of CDs and CDILs. TU-1810DSPC UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to characterize the UV–vis absorption of CDs and CDILs. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the CDILs was recorded with a TG simultaneous thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, STA449F3).

The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, MERLIN Compact) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used to detect the morphologies and chemical compositions of the worn surface. The acceleration voltage of EDS analysis is 15 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was operated to further analyze the composition of the worn surfaces with monochromatized Al Ka as the X-ray source. Cross-sectional TEM sample of worn surface with the Pt protection layer was made by the focused ion beam (FIB). Specially, after friction test, the wear scar was wiped with ethanol. The steel disk was put in the SEM sample room to seek the slice position. Then, the Pt coating protect film was generated on the slice position by the ion sputtering. Finally, the TEM sample was made by the FIB method. And the HR-TEM images and EDS elemental mapping of cross-sectional TEM sample were obtained by the FEI Talos-F200S.

The lubricating properties of CDILs and [BMIM][BF4] as lubricants were investigated by using an Optimol SRV-V oscillating reciprocating friction and wear tester with a ball-on-disk frictional pair. The upper running ball (Φ10 mm) and the lower stationary disk (Φ24 mm × 8.0 mm) were made by the GCr15 bearing steel. The friction and wear tests were conducted at the test conditions of 30 °C and 100 °C, frequency: 25 Hz, amplitude: 1.0 mm, load: 200 N, time: 30 min. The 30 min includes pre-grinding time (50 N) for 30 s, load time (50 N to 200 N) and test time at 200 N. The friction curves only have the test time period. The MicroXAM-800 3D surface mapping microscope profilometer was used to quantify the wear volume of lower steel disk. Moreover, a long-term friction test was also done with [N888H][CDs] for 2 h at 100 °C under the load of 200 N to explore the lubrication mechanism of CDILs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of CDs and CDILs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2
a and 2b show TEM morphology and HR-TEM image of CDs, demonstrating that the uniform quasi-spherical CDs with an average size of about 2.2 nm without obvious aggregation were synthesized successfully. And the prepared CDs are amorphous without lattice distance, for the reason of the bottom-up method with pyrolysis and shorter pyrolysis time, which is a common phenomenon for some CDs [39,40]. Moreover, the chemical structures of CDs and CDILs were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3 . The peaks of CDs located at 3450 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 are concerned with the stretching vibration of the OH bond in hydroxy groups and the CO bond in carboxyl groups, respectively. Furthermore, nearly all the peaks (<1500 cm−1) of CDs have disappeared relative to the spectrum of CA. The results above suggest that CA molecules have been decomposed and plenty of hydroxy and carboxyl groups exist on the surface of CDs. Taking [N888H][CDs] for example, the spectrum implies that the stretching vibrations of CH at 2952 cm−1 and 2864 cm−1 are stronger, illustrating the existence of cationic alkyl chains. Simultaneously, as the new bands, the stretching vibrations of the amine salt appearing at 2250–3000 cm−1 and the peaks at 1378 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretches of the carboxylate groups are detected. The deformation vibrations of CH2 at 722 cm−1 is the mark of four continuous methylene groups in [N888H][CDs]. The peaks of new bonds above indicate the successful synthesis of CDILs.

The UV–vis spectra of CA, CDs and CDILs are shown in Fig. 4
a (the solvent of [N888H][CDs] is ethanol and others are deionized water), in which CA exhibits a strong absorption below 230 nm. By contrast, the peaks at 246 nm and 330 nm of CDs are attributed to the π-π* transitions of the CC bonds and the n-π* transitions of the CO bonds, respectively. Similarly, the UV–vis absorption peaks above 230 nm also appear in the spectra of CDILs. The aqueous solutions of CDs possessing the fluorescence property glow blue-green light under a hand-held UV lamp.

The thermal stability of three CDILs, CDs and [BMIM][BF4] was measured using TGA (Fig. 4 b). The temperature of 10 % mass loss of [N444H][CDs], [N4444][CDs] and [N888H][CDs] are about 106 °C, 156 °C and 168 °C, respectively. The thermal stability of [N888H][CDs] is better than that of other CDILs. Nevertheless, [N444H][CDs] decomposes at 106 °C, which might affect the lubricating application at high temperature. To evaluate the stability of CDILs over time, they were placed in the oven at the 30 and 100 °C. As shown in Fig. S4 , CDILs have no obvious change after placing one day and one week at 30 °C. At 100 °C for one day, CDILs could maintain the good liquidity and exterior color. However, as a result of oxidation, the color becomes darker after setting them one week. Especially [N444H][CDs], the liquidity declines significantly for one week, also illustrating worse thermal stability.

The friction coefficient curves of lubricants are depicted in Fig. 5
a. The friction coefficient of [BMIM][BF4] slowly increases in the beginning and then becomes smooth to the end. Whereas, three CDILs exhibit smoother and steadier friction coefficient during the whole friction period. The friction reduction effects of [N4444][CDs] and [N888H][CDs] are better than [N444H][CDs], being attributed to the length and number of alkyl chains for cations.

Fig. 5 b shows that the anti-wear properties of CDILs are superior to that of [BMIM][BF4]. The wear volume reduction of [N4444][CDs] is over 90 %. Excitingly, the wear scars of [N888H][CDs] and [N444H][CDs] are quite shallow so that the profilometer cannot detect the wear volume. The acquired results reveal that three CDILs perform good lubricating behaviors and unexpected anti-wear effects at room temperature.

For [BMIM][BF4], as a result of electron emissions from the friction surface, the anions prefer to adsorb on the friction surface and then intense tribochemical reactions may occur by severe friction conditions, which will produce a mass of tribochemical products to provide good lubricating behaviors [32]. However, the anion BF4 − is very sensitive to moisture and may hydrolyze to generate HF, which will erode substrate [35]. The intense tribochemical reactions and corrosion may lead to corrosion wear. The corrosion products generated on the substrate could exhibit the friction-reduction effects. Hence, [BMIM][BF4] exhibits lower friction and higher wear. As shown in Fig. 6
a and 6b, signs of severe adhesive wear and plastic deformation are found on the wear scar surface of the disk lubricated by [BMIM][BF4]. For CDILs, there are no corrosive elements in the molecular structure which could not cause corrosion wear. In addition, the higher viscosity might result in a thicker lubricating film. Thus, CDILs could exhibit lower friction and slighter wear during the friction process. Thus, Fig. 6 c to h provide sufficient evidence that the adhesive wear and plastic deformation have disappeared, and only some shallow furrows exist on the surface lubricated by [N4444][CDs]. Furthermore, the lubricated surface of [N888H][CDs] and [N444H][CDs] is even smoother and flatter.

For the purpose of investigating the lubricating properties of CDILs at high temperature, test temperature was elevated to 100 °C under the same load of 200 N. The friction curves and wear volume of all lubricants at 100 °C are given in Fig. 7 . It is observed in Fig. 7 a that friction coefficients of all the oil samples are stable without significant fluctuations. Besides, [N888H][CDs] maintains the great friction-reducing effect at high temperature, and provides the lowest friction coefficient. As shown in Fig. 7 b, the wear volume of [BMIM][BF4] is the highest, but the wear volume of [N888H][CDs] and [N4444][CDs] is still extremely low at high temperature. At high temperature, the decreased viscosity of CDILs reduces the lubricating film quality, for which the wear volume of CDILs increases compared with that at 30 °C. In addition, the wear volume of [N444H][CDs] is the highest among the three CDILs at the 100 °C, even close to [BMIM][BF4]. This probably because [N444H][CDs] decomposes initially at the temperature of 100 °C (Fig. 4 b). Herein the structures of [N444H][CDs] may be destroyed at high temperature.

The SEM micrographs of wear scars in Fig. 8 are consistent with the results of wear volume. It is obvious that severe roughness grooves and deep furrows generate on the worn surface lubricated by [BMIM][BF4] at high temperature. However, the CDILs exhibit better lubrication performance than [BMIM][BF4] by giving smoother worn surface. For the [N888H][CDs] and [N444H][CDs], wear scars are more obvious in contrast to that at 30 °C, illustrating that the anti-wear performance decreases when the temperature goes up. Together with the data at the two temperatures, these results illustrate that the different cation structures play a significant role in the lubricating properties of CDILs in spite of the same anion.

In order to further reveal the lubrication mechanism of CDILs, a long-period friction test was carried out with [N888H][CDs] for 2 h at 100 °C under a load of 200 N. Fig. 9 shows stable friction coefficient during the whole sliding process, implying the steady and continuous tribofilm on the worn surface. The EDS analysis results (Table 2 and Table S1 ) of worn surface manifest that the concentration of elements has no obvious change for different sliding time (1800 s and 7200 s) and the carbon content of worn surface is higher than that of unworn surface. Furthermore, there is no N element being detected on the worn surface. However, the N element could be detected in the XPS spectra (Fig. 10
and
Fig. S5 ), which might be due to the lower content in the tribofilm. In the Table S2 , the atomic contents obtained by the XPS analysis are also displayed, indicating that the variation trend of C and O element is consistent with EDS results. As presented in Fig. 10, XPS spectra provide component analyses of worn surface lubricated by [N888H][CDs] for 2 h. The C 1s peaks at 284.79 eV, 285.54 eV and 288.29 eV are assigned to the CC/CC of CDs and cationic alkyl chains, CO/CN and CO, respectively [41]. For the N 1s peaks located at 399.41 eV and 400.68 eV, which is attributed to the CN of the cation molecules [42,43]. In addition, the worn surface contains iron oxides which could be verified by the O 1s (529.98 eV) and Fe 2p spectra. Combined with EDS analysis results, there are small amounts of O element involved in the tribochemical reactions. The XPS and EDS results imply that the tribofilm contained C, O, N and Fe elements is formed by the adsorption and tribochemical reactions between CDILs and steel substrate.

The cross-sectional TEM images and EDS analyses are shown in Fig. 11 . Through the full view (Fig. 11 a), three parts emerge in the cross profile, including the Pt protective layer, the tribofilm and the steel substrate. The middle layer displays a continuous thick film with an average thickness of about 240 nm. Moreover, the metal oxide layer is inconspicuous, suggesting that the tribofilm is compact and strong which can prevent oxidization (Fig. 11 d and 11f). It is noted in Fig. 11 e that the tribofilm is mainly composed of amorphous nanoparticles with a size of 2 nm, which is supposed to be the CDs in this tribosystem (Fig. 2). To verify the elemental composition and distribution of the tribofilm, further EDS analyses were conducted, and results in Fig. 11 c show that the tribofilm is mainly composed of C, N, O and Fe elements. It can be found from Fig. 11 d that higher C element content and lower N element content are in tribofilm, and the quantitative relation of them is complementary. The results afford compelling evidence that the CDs embedded in the tribofilm are basic ingredients, and the decomposed products of CDILs participate in the construction of then protective film. In the light of the surface and cross-section EDS analyses, it further discloses that the adsorption of the CD anions is the main reason for forming the tribofilm.

Based on the above results, we propose that the tribological protective film is mainly made up of the CD anions, and the mechanism diagrams are described in Fig. 12 . Similar to previous reports [10,12,41], the adsorption of CDs is the critical step in the formation of the protective carbon tribofilm. However, the difference in this work is that CDs are the anions of ILs, which can reinforce the adsorption on the friction surface to generate a firmer, thicker and more compact film. Moreover, the tribofilm grown here can also prevent the oxidation of the steel surface, which can be well proved by the inconspicuous oxide layer, what also further confirms the compactness of the carbon film layer. What’s more, the carbon dots as nanoparticles could provide the mending effect during the rubbing process to decrease the wear. At room temperature, high-viscosity CDILs without corrosive elements could prevent substrate from corrosive wear. With the temperature increasing, the film thickness will change as a result of the lower viscosity, resulting in direct contact of the friction pair easily. In this case, CDILs could adsorb on the steel surface and then occur the tribochemical reactions to form the carbon-based tribofilm. In particular, for [N888H][CDs], it has the great thermal stability due to the long alkyl chains, which could remain lubrication performance at high temperature. In summary, the CDs anionic moieties and ammonium cationic moieties together form a protective lubricating film during the sliding process, to prevent the oxidization and obtain excellent lubrication performance.

In this work, three novel carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs) were synthesized through ionic modification of CDs with organic ammonium cations. The lubricating properties and the lubrication mechanism of CDILs as neat liquid lubricants were evaluated and conclusions could be drawn as follow:
(1)
Three CDILs exhibit the liquid status at room temperature, which promises them widespread application as liquid lubricants in field of lubrication engineering.
(2)
At the 30 °C, CDILs perform good friction-reducing property and excellent anti-wear performance. Especially for [N444H][CDs] and [N888H][CDs], they manifest inconspicuous wear track on the steel surface, which are distinctly better than classical ILs [BMIM][BF4].
(3)
The wear resistance ability of CDILs decreases a little when the test temperature is elevated to 100 °C, which are still superior to classical ILs [BMIM][BF4]. Additionally, [N888H][CDs] shows the excellent friction-reduction and anti-wear properties both at room temperature and high temperature.
(4)
A firmer, thicker and more compact tribofilm mainly composed of amorphous CDs should endow the dominating lubrication mechanism of CDILs in this work. During the friction process, the CDs anionic moieties and ammonium cationic moieties together form a protective lubricating film to prevent the oxidization and obtain excellent lubrication performance.
(5)
The present work provides a novel and simple method to modify the CDs. Simultaneously, newly synthesized CDILs exhibit the high-performance in the field of lubrication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51975559), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2020ME135)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.121458.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K.Q. Luo, P. Chen, Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications, Small 11(14) (2015) 1620-1636.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications
